question_text:
The list of first presidential electors needs to include:
General Samuel G. Clark (born 17 Feb 1741 in Westchester County, New York and died 17 Feb 1823 at age 82 in Malta, Saratoga County, New York) was one of the first presidential electors from the District, November 20, 1792. He voted for George Washington at his re-election. He was a presidential elector in 1796 when Federalist John Adams won and Democratic-Republican Thomas Jefferson became vice-president (the position of vice-president went to the person with the second highest number of votes until that process changed in 1804). Clark voted for George Clinton, former Governor from New York, in 1805-1809, who was running for vice-president with Thomas Jefferson against Aaron Burr.
Clark was commissioned Lieutenant by General Washington at White Plains; named Captain on June 16, 1778 and Major on May 6, 1779, both at Poughkeepsie; Lieutenant-Colonel on May 19, 1782, and Major on Oct 11, 1786, both at East Line, Saratoga Co.; Brigadier General of the 9th Brigade of Saratoga Co. on July 3, 1804; and became a Major General by March 8, 1814.
Clark served as a Judge and held the Court of General Sessions of Saratoga County in his home on May 10, 1781. He served as a Justice of the Peace (1791-1809), as County Treasurer (1792-1794), and as a Member of State Assembly (1796-1799 and 1800-1805).
His family attended the Presbyterian Church in Ballston Spa until around 1810, but he was involved with the Methodist Church. He died on his 82nd birthday in 1823, in Malta, Saratoga, New York and was buried in Ballston Center, Saratoga, New York.

question_text: Hi, I want to create pages for some of living legends but they are not familiar with Wikipedia. to start this first I want to creat a page for myself, not a legend but want to understand the process. can you help and guide me how to do it
reply_text:
Hi @! I wouldn't recommend [POLICY], as you have a [POLICY], and probably don't meet the [POLICY] we require of people to have stand alone articles. However, if you have other subjects here's the steps to take:
# Ask yourself whether they meet [POLICY] (primarily, do they have significant coverage in [POLICY], independent, [POLICY]?)
# if the subject does, I would begin by creating a draft through [POLICY]. If you navigate to that link, there will be a blue button that says "Click here to start a new article". Click it! And you can create a draft page start writing your article, and submit it for review when it is 'done'.
# Make sure the article is cited to reliable sources, written from a [POLICY], and [POLICY].
For help with this, I would recommend reading [HELP_PAGE], and looking at existing articles on similar topics to see how they are written. [POLICY] is an interactive guide to the *basics* of editing, and if you have any further questions, feel free to ask them here or at [POLICY]! There are a lot of friendly editors happy to help you out. You can also experiment with editing on the draft you create (in step 2) without any major repercussions, or at [POLICY]. One of the best ways to learn how to edit is by doing it! We're here to help you through the inevitable mistakes
Happy editing,

question_text: Hello David, I have been a professor of music and while I have wider interests, most of the articles I read related to music and musicians. How do I start with an article that I remember needed some editing?
reply_text:
Cscottfree21 Within certain limits (some articles are semi-locked or locked from edits by new or newish accounts), anyone can edit any article. Keep in mind that any addition of facts requires verification via published references. For musicians (and any other people articles), what that person puts on their own website or social media or press releases or interviews are not considered independent references. General advice is be bold in your edits, but if reverted (reversed), start a discussion on the Talk page of the article in a goal of reaching consensus. For each edit, include a brief Edit summary in the space provided at the bottom, to describe what you did.
See [POLICY] and [POLICY] for some guidelines.

question_text: How do I create a citation
reply_text: Hi @ the simplest thing to do is put your reference information inside "ref" tags, like this: [REFERENCE CONTENT HERE] Place that code at the end of the sentence you want to attach the citation to, and the reference will show exactly what you've typed in. At a very basic level, that's all your need. Now, the second step is that we have templates for citations, like [TEMPLATE_REF], which do all the fancy formatting for you. If you copy the template, and fill in the various parameters, you'll get a full citation automatically. So the full (blank) code would be this: [CITATION] Just fill that in with the information you have (or use [TEMPLATE_REF] or [TEMPLATE_REF] if appropriate) and you're good to go. Let me know what follow-up questions you have.

question_text: How can I delate my post?
reply_text: Hi @, in most cases reverting the edit or otherwise removing the content is sufficient and often the only appropriate method of removal (in the interest of record keeping). In some limited circumstances, the page or a specific revision of the page can be deleted by administrators (although it still be accessed by admins and undeleted if necessary). See the [POLICY] and [POLICY] for appropriate reasons for deletion. question_text:
Hello Alyo,
I am a new Wikipedia editor. I thought of a couple of questions before starting:
1) Do references need to be used in the text to be listed in the References section?
2) If I add text in the middle of an existing entry, with a new reference, will references later in the text be renumbered automatically?
Thanks in advance for sharing your guidance!
PSU-TRS88
reply_text: Hi @, these are great questions! To answer simply: yes (generally) to both of your questions. When you edit wikis, there are certain types of code that will automatically do most of this for you. You should read [POLICY], but when you use ... tags (with the citation in between the tags), the citation will automatically be numbered and added to a list at the end of the page. The list is generated wherever the template is placed, so just make sure that template exists--it's usually in the References section.

question_text: Hello! I'm so grateful that you are here to help me. I've started an article in draft form, but it's going to take me some time to get it substantively ready, let alone format it. I have a number of citations that I have not added yet. My first question is this: should I include multiple citations if available for a single factual statement or is it better to limit citations to one per factual statement? Thank you, Bunnypranav.
reply_text:
Hi, it’s usually better to limit references and only use as many as needed, choosing the strongest source (see [POLICY] for some help on deciding that) - but in some situations, using multiple references can be helpful. If sources differ, multiple references may be needed to demonstrate that the factual statement is the most common/prevailing version. Or, an event that has been live-streamed may benefit from a reference to the stream in addition to RS reporting on the event, for another situation.
In terms of subjective statements, multiple references may be needed to demonstrate its [POLICY], i.e. that the opinion is common enough to be important.

question_text:
Hello! Nice to meet you here!
I want to write an article here on Wikipedia about my startup company, EasyDMARC, which is the leading in the cybersecurity sphere.
From where to start? Can you help me?
reply_text:
Hi @,
I would very strongly advise against it. It can be very difficult to get any article accepted, and even more so when you're writing about something you have an external relationship with, such as your business. The slightest hint of promotional content or tone, and the draft won't even be reviewed, it will most likely just get speedily deleted.
You then have to make sure that you don't write whatever it is that you yourself want to say about your business, but rather summarise what independent and reliable secondary sources have said — see [POLICY] for advice on what sort of coverage we need to see. This may seem like an impossible hurdle, especially as you say your business is only a startup, but without citing sources that are good enough to meet the GNG criteria, your article simply will not be accepted.
If, despite all that, you still want to go ahead, the very first thing you need to do is to disclose your conflict of interest. I note that another editor has already placed a message on with instructions for how to do this. Please follow those instructions exactly.
Best,

First, is the subject you are editing closely related to yourself, family, friends, clients, employers, financial or other relationships (having a [POLICY] or COI)?
Before creating a new article, you must make sure that the subject is [POLICY] for inclusion. Text on Wikipedia must be [POLICY] by [POLICY] so that information is [POLICY]erifiable. Also, [POLICY], as [POLICY] on Wikipedia.
When you're ready to create a new article, go to [HELP_PAGE], which will guide you through creating a new article. You should go through the entire page before proceeding to the Article Wizard. After creating a new article draft, it will be reviewed by an [POLICY] volunteer. Be patient; there are currently , and they are not reviewed in any specific order. In the meantime, you might want to do some [POLICY] so that the article is more likely to be approved. If you have more questions, don't hesitate to ask me using your or at the [POLICY] and learn about editing Wikipedia through our [HELP_PAGE]. Happy editing! ~~

To learn how to edit, I suggest you start at [HELP_PAGE], and then spend a significant amount of time editing existing articles to hone your skills. Once you're ready to create an article, you would gather multiple [POLICY] that have provided [POLICY] of the subject, and determine whether it meets Wikipedia's criteria for inclusion, called "[POLICY]". If so, you could follow the instructions at [HELP_PAGE] and summarize what the sources have published. Be prepared for a process that may include waiting for review, declines, and rewrites before an article is accepted. Hope this helps, and happy editing!

question_text: Hi Robert, my client wants to change her picture on wikipedia. What is the best way to do this?
reply_text:
@:
# While you are editing on English Wikipedia, do take note of [POLICY] policy, especially on paid editing and disclosing one's COI.
# As for the new image, if she holds the copyright to the image she intends to change to, it is best to have it [WIKILINK], our sister project for hosting copyright-free images. In doing so, you will have to declare that the photo is to be released to [WIKILINK]. To ensure that the photo will not be deleted, you can confirm permission by using the [WIKILINK] to send it via [WIKILINK]. It is best that after the upload to Commons is done and verified, you then switch the photo in the article. Note that if the image is found to be unacceptable for the article, the edit may be reverted. It may be better to add [TEMPLATE_REF] in a new thread/topic on the article's talk page to alert passing editors on the pending change you would like to make.
# Type of photo for BLPs (assuming is it a biography article): clear portrait shot of the face is sufficient. See also [POLICY].

reply_text:
Hello Aikam Janda, and welcome to Wikipedia! (Sorry for the wait, seems that Oshwah's probably on a break.) This is an [HELP_PAGE], which is when someone else publish their changes while you are still editing.
To resolve an edit conflict, you will need to essentially "merge" both your and the other editor's changes. The edit conflict page shows the [POLICY] between your and the other editor's changes, the currently published version of the page for you to edit, and a copy of your changes.
To make resolving edit conflicts easier, you can turn on "Paragraph-based edit conflicts" under . ~~

question_text:
hello i edited an article before registering an account, how can I link those edits to this account now?
the IP address is 2001:1C06:1B08:AA00:E96D:5EBB:6578:196F
reply_text:
Hi @! Welcome to Wikipedia! Thank you for getting in touch. Unfortunately, as far as I know, there's currently no way to officially link the edits to your account, or add them to . There's also no requirement for you to do this, unless you want to, as disclosing your IP address can be a privacy issue.
Have a look at [POLICY], which tells you what you can do if you do want to publicly make it clear in the article's edit history that you made those edits before you logged in; as that page says, one way of doing this is by making a [HELP_PAGE] (an edit that doesn't visibly change the content of the page, but allows you to leave an edit summary).
For instance, you could go to the article you edited (from the of the IP address you gave, it looks like that is the article for [WIKILINK]), click [LINK], and paste the following text anywhere in the article: (the and --> make this text an invisible "comment" within the article's source code).
You can then add an [HELP_PAGE] (see [HELP_PAGE] for instructions on how to do that) stating that you made the previous edits from an IP address while logged out of your account; you could say something like: dummy edit, made previous edits as IP address 2001:1C06:1B08:AA00:E96D:5EBB:6578:196F while logged out.
Then, click "Publish changes" and your edit will be published. Afterwards, you can click "Edit" again, delete , and leave an edit summary saying something like deleting dummy edit. I hope this is helpful! Let me know if you have any other questions. :)

Yes, there are two sandboxes: there's the [POLICY], which is used by all users. Whatever you put on their will get deleted by someone else fairly quickly, so be warned. There's also , which will save as nobody should (hopefully) be touching it but you. Your sandbox currently does not exist as a page, but if you click the red link then it should be made.
Thanks for expressing interest in editing Wikipedia! Best of luck! ( · )

question_text: Hey, hope you're alright please how do I edit the title of pages.
reply_text:
@: Once you have ten edits and your account is at least four days old you will gain the ability to "move" pages. Please note that the use of this tool requires the move to either be uncontroversial or be backed by consensus. If the move may be controversial or requires consensus, you can post at [POLICY].
Happy editing! ~ ~

question_text: Hello - I am trying to change the page name from "WE Communications" to our new company name "We. Communications". Can you help me figure out how to do that? Thanks much!
reply_text: Hello Sunagurol2, and welcome to Wikipedia! Here, renaming a page is called "moving" it. It looks like there's been some disagreement about what this article should be titled, so I started an official discussion about it [WIKILINK]. It would be helpful if you would read [POLICY], especially [POLICY], and then give a brief comment (1 sentence is enough!) at that article about what you think the best format of the title could be. Also, if you work for this company, you should read our guidelines about [POLICY], and especially make sure you have disclosed the connection somewhere. Let me know if you have any questions.

Note that if there is likelihood of somebody objecting to the new title, you should start a discussion on the article talk page to establish [POLICY] before moving. Is there a particular page you think needs to at a new title?

question_text: Hi Mathglot, I am an expert in reproductive physiology and translational fertility. If I found an error in the Wiki article, what is the best way to fix it: myself or reach out to an editor? Thanks
reply_text:
Hi, LastLegend385, and welcome! The best way is to fix it yourself. No expertise needed, btw, just an ability to [HELP_PAGE], and knowledge of how to create Wikipedia-style [POLICY]. Recommend you have a look at the suite of [TEMPLATE_REF], such as , , (there are many others in the series). You may always choose to make an [POLICY] instead, but the only time when that is strongly encouraged is when you have an [POLICY] of some sort, and very definitely if you are [POLICY] by any entity relate to the article you want to edit. Other than that, just [POLICY]!
By the way, experts in a field who are new to Wikipedia very often fall into the trap of adding content to articles without including a [POLICY] to a [POLICY] because they know the topic backwards and forwards and could expound for hours about it without cracking a book. Please don't do that. Even if you wrote the definitive textbook about a topic, you need to cite it (well, cite somebody else's, just to avoid a suspicion of [POLICY]). Always cite assertions to a source; grammar changes, typos, rewording for clarity, etc., don't require citations. Cheers,

question_text:
Hello Eddie891, I have just now created this account but did some editing on Wiki around 5 years ago. I use the encyclopedia often but am retired and my usage is leisurely.
I was reading the Human Migration page. While there's a lot of good information, the page needs a major revamping. There are contradictions between the sections, the logic is weak, and there's a jumble of conflicting definitions. The result is confusion. I checked the 'talk' on this page, and found that back in 2015/17 someone else made similar points. They suggested a "Merge". I suggest a reshuffle and cutting. The topic needs to be precisely defined, logical connection between sections made, and then the links to other Wiki pages clearly specified. The way it stands now, this page tries to do everything and ends up doing nothing.
This is an important topic. Please have a look at it. I could help revise but it would mean a total reshuffle.
Thanks for you consideration on this,
Jansan72
reply_text: Hi, @, thanks for the note. Unfortunately, I don't have the bandwidth to work on this article at the moment. I'd suggest leaving a note like this on the talk page and hoping someone picks it up as your best option. Cheers,

question_text: How do I access editing on the article "List of NEON Films"?
reply_text: 6218946rr That page is [POLICY] so that only extended-confirmed users can edit it. This protection expires on 18 November 2024. You can either wait for the protection to expire, or wait until you have the extended-confirmed permission (500 edits, 30 days old). Additionally, you can make an [POLICY] on the [WIKILINK] and someone can make the changes for you. Thanks! — (; )

question_text:
Hello! I've used wiki for years and have occasionally seen what i think of as omissions from an article. What prompted me to create an account today are omissions from the article on Generating Functions. More specifically, I would like to make suggestions for the section on multivariate generating functions. To get started, let me be _very_ specific: I believe it would be good to include the generating function form of Bayes Theorem. How -- and to whom -- do i communicate this suggestion?
Thank you, btw, for your help.
reply_text: Hi, you don’t need to find the right person, Wikipedia doesn’t work like that. Everything happens transparently on the article itself. Please post your suggestion on the article’s Talk page with reliable sources supporting the edit.

reply_text: Observer.physio Good day. Please note that all new page reviewers are volunteers who review articles in their own time and based on their interests. There are currently about ten thousand new articles in the backlog. Therefore, it might take a day or two, or even up to three months, for an article to be reviewed. Kindly remain patient in the meantime, and thank you for your contribution. Stay safe.

question_text: Hi Cullen. I want to use the Draft function in the following way. Rather than produce a draft from scratch, I want the draft's starting point to be an existing article, such as the Ethics article. Can that be done?
reply_text: Hello, ForeverBetter. That is not the purpose of draft space, which is to draft new articles. You can use your personal sandbox space to draft new content for existing articles. As for [WIKILINK], it is not a good idea in almost all cases to do a dramatic rewrite of an article that many editors have contributed to over 24 years. Perhaps you should describe your plans for the article at [WIKILINK] first.
